instruction: Extract all core computer science concepts from the following lecture JSON. Return only a newline-separated list of unique concepts. Do not explain anything.

images.ai_description: A cartoonish octopus manipulates technology, pouring binary code from containers, illustrating data processing and the complexity of information technology systems in a whimsical manner.

input.title: Memory Allocation Example
input.notes:
big_array, huge_array, global:  data 
int local,

input.title: Memory Allocation Example
input.notes:
big_array, huge_array, global:  data 
Useless, main: instructions
P1, p2, p3, p4, local: stack (or registers)
Malloc’d data: on the heap (p[1..4] are pointers, on the stack, to the data on the heap)

input.title: Reminder:  x86-64/Linux Stack Frame

text_blocks.text: Local variables(if can’t be kept in registers)
“Argument build” area(If callee needs to call another function -parameters for function about to call, if needed)

runs.text: needs to call another function -parameters for function about to call, if needed)

input.notes: Frame pointer %rbp points to the bottom of the current function’s stack (not always used – was more important back in the days when assembly was hand-written)

input.title: Buffer Overflow

text_blocks.text: Traditional Linux memory layout provide opportunities for malicious programs

runs.text: Traditional Linux memory layout provide opportunities for malicious programs

text_blocks.text: Data and instructions both stored in the same memory

runs.text: Data and instructions both stored in the same memory

text_blocks.text: Recall that C does not check array bounds

runs.text: Recall that C does not check array bounds

text_blocks.text: Many Unix/Linux/C functions don’t check argument sizes

runs.text: Many Unix/Linux/C functions don’t check argument sizes

text_blocks.text: Allows overflowing (writing past the end) of buffers (arrays)

runs.text: Allows overflowing (writing past the end) of buffers (arrays)

input.notes:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morris_worm
Stack grows backwards: meaning you can write too much data onto the top of the stack, and begin overwriting stuff that was already there!

text_blocks.text: Buffer overflows on the stack can overwrite “interesting” data

runs.text: Buffer overflows on the stack can overwrite “interesting” data

text_blocks.text: Attackers just choose the right inputs

runs.text: Attackers just choose the right inputs

text_blocks.text: Unchecked length on string input into bounded array causes overwriting of stack data

runs.text: Unchecked length on string input into bounded array causes overwriting of stack data

runs.text: Try to change the return address of the current procedure

runs.text: Why is this a big deal?

text_blocks.text: #1 overall cause is social engineering / user ignorance

runs.text: #1 overall cause is social engineering / user ignorance

input.title: Vulnerable Buffer Code

text_blocks.text: /* Echo Line */void echo() {    char buf[8];  /* Way too small! */    gets(buf);    puts(buf);}
Code example to try on Thoth!

runs.text: Code example to try on Thoth!

input.notes:
If we overrun the buffer, it’s fine for a while...
But then we add one more character, and it seg-faults!
Show on Attu if time (1-9, 0-9, 0123 4 causes segfault)
Why? (let’s see...)
Label: input buffer, gets() reads input, puts() writes output

input.title: Buffer Overflow Disassembly

text_blocks.text: 4006a3:	e8 a5 ff ff ff       	callq  40064d <gets>
4006be:	e8 d9 ff ff ff       	callq  40069c <echo>

input.notes:
@plt means grab from “procedure linkage table” (specific to dynamic linking and loading)
0x18 = 24 in decimal
Call pushes the return address onto the stack
Printf call for writing “enter string”

text_blocks.text: /* Echo Line */void echo(){    char buf[4];  /* Way too small! */    gets(buf);    puts(buf);}
Overflowed buffer, but did not corrupt state

runs.text: Overflowed buffer, but did not corrupt state

input.title: Buffer Overflow Example #2 Explained

text_blocks.text: Eventually segfaults on retq of deregister_tm_clones.

input.notes: deregister_tm_clones deals with transactional memory, which is intended to make programming with threads simpler (parallelism and synchronization – waaaaaay beyond the scope of this course).

input.notes:
Overwrite A with a stack address
Encode malicious code into the buffer
Return to your code, which can execute a program like bash
You now have whatever permissions the vulnerable program had!

input.title: Quiz

text_blocks.text: smash_me is vulnerable to stack smashing!
What is the minimum number of characters that gets must read for us to change the return address to a stack address (in Linux)?

runs.text: must read for us to change the return address to a

input.notes:
Stack addresses always start with 0x00007FFF…
Little endian, so the most significant bits are at the highest address (upper left)
0x30 = 48 in decimal
Need to overwrite those 48 bytes + 6 bytes of the return address (since the stack is much higher up than instructions)
So the answer is 48 + 6 = 54

text_blocks.text: Use library routines that limit string lengths

runs.text: Use library routines that limit string lengths

text_blocks.text: fgets instead of gets (2nd argument to fgets sets limit)
Don’t use scanf with %s conversion specification
Use fgets to read the string
Or use %ns where n is a suitable integer
/* Echo Line */void echo(){    char buf[8];  /* Way too small! */    fgets(buf, 8, stdin);    puts(buf);}
At start of program, allocate random amount of space on stack
Shifts stack addresses for entire program

runs.text: Shifts stack addresses for entire program

text_blocks.text: Addresses will vary from one run to another

runs.text: Addresses will vary from one run to another

text_blocks.text: Makes it difficult for hacker to predict beginning of inserted code

runs.text: Makes it difficult for hacker to predict beginning of inserted code

input.title: Protected Buffer Disassembly (buf)
input.notes:
%fs:0x28 generates some ”random” data
Put it on the stack above local variables
At end of function, check whether it changed

Read canary value
Store on stack
Erase from register
Read current canary from stack
Compare against orig. value
Return if unchanged
“stack smashing detected”
